title: Mizuho OSI Trios Surgical Table System - Table Top Not Communicating with Base
description: Identifies causes of table top communication failure with base, including cabling, connector, power, and control interface issues before assuming internal electronics failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not supporting a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Power to Table Top and Base
steps.instructions: Verify both table top and base are powered. Check power cords, plugs, and outlet functionality. No power can prevent communication.

steps.title: Check Connection Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect the communication cable between table top and base for secure attachment, damage, or corrosion. Reseat or replace cable if necessary.

steps.title: Verify Connector Orientation
steps.instructions: Ensure connectors are fully seated in the correct orientation. Misaligned connectors can prevent proper data exchange.

steps.title: Check Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Observe status LEDs on the table top and base. Blinking or off lights may indicate connection, power, or fault issues.

steps.title: Test Hand Control/Interface Panel
steps.instructions: Operate table functions via hand control or interface panel. Lack of response may reinforce a communication failure.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Table Top (if available)
steps.instructions: If possible, connect a verified working table top to the base. Communication restores function indicates the issue is with the original table top.

CCR.Complaint: Table top not responding or communicating with base.
CCR.Cause: Communication cable was loose, preventing data exchange between table top and base.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated cable and verified proper connection; table functions restored.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: operational after cable reseating

title: Mizuho OSI Trios Surgical Table System - Movement Functions Inoperative or Limited Range
description: Identifies causes of inoperative or limited table movement, including power, control, connection, actuator, and safety lock issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not supporting a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the table is plugged into a live, tested outlet. Inspect power cords for damage or loose connections. Movement will not function without proper power.

steps.title: Check Battery or Backup Power
steps.instructions: If the table has a battery, ensure it is charged and properly seated. Limited movement can occur if battery voltage is low.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control and Pendant
steps.instructions: Confirm the hand control or pendant is properly connected and undamaged. Test buttons or switches for responsiveness. A faulty control can prevent table movement.

steps.title: Examine Connection Between Base and Table Top
steps.instructions: Ensure all connectors between the base and the table top are fully seated. Loose or damaged connections can inhibit movement.

steps.title: Check Safety Locks or Brakes
steps.instructions: Verify that all mechanical locks, brakes, or actuators are disengaged. Engaged locks may restrict or prevent movement.

steps.title: Test Movement Functions Individually
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate each movement function (height, tilt, lateral, Trendelenburg) separately. Note any movement, unusual resistance, sounds, or partial function.

steps.title: Inspect for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Look under and around the table for any items or cables obstructing movement. Even small obstructions can limit range or prevent actuation.

CCR.Complaint: Surgical table movement functions inoperative or limited.
CCR.Cause: Hand control and base-to-top connections verified; mechanical locks engaged and obstructing travel.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared obstructions and disengaged locks; functions restored and table tested.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual resistance or sounds during testing
Final device status (operational, limited, or out of service)

title: Mizuho OSI Trios Surgical Table System - Power or Battery Failure
description: Identifies causes of power or battery failure, including power source, cord, battery, and connection issues before assuming internal electronics failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not supporting a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify External Power Source
steps.instructions:
Check that the outlet is live using a known working device.
Inspect the power cord for damage or loose connections.
Plug the table directly into a verified outlet (avoid extension cords).
Expected Outcome: Table powers on or battery begins charging.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Status
steps.instructions:
Locate the table’s battery pack(s).
Check for loose connections or visible damage.
Confirm battery charge indicator (if available).
Expected Outcome: Battery shows charge or begins charging when connected to power.

steps.title: Swap or Recharge Battery
steps.instructions:
If a spare or replacement battery is available, install it to verify the issue.
Ensure charging station or built-in charger is functioning.
Expected Outcome: Table powers on with a known good battery.

steps.title: Check Control Panel/Hand Pendant Response
steps.instructions:
Attempt to power on using the main control panel and hand pendant.
Observe any LED indicators, error codes, or alarm beeps.
Expected Outcome: Table responds to commands, or error code indicates battery/power fault.

steps.title: Inspect Fuses and Circuit Breakers
steps.instructions:
If accessible, check table fuses and any onboard circuit breakers.
Replace blown fuses with manufacturer-specified types only.
Expected Outcome: Table powers on or fault clears.

CCR.Complaint: Table will not power on; battery does not charge.
CCR.Cause: Battery pack faulty or external power source interrupted.
CCR.Resolution: Tested outlet and power cord; swapped battery with known good pack; table powers on.

title: Mizuho OSI Trios Surgical Table System - Locking Mechanism Not Engaging
description: Identifies causes of the table’s locking mechanism failing to engage, including power, control, actuator, or safety interlock issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not supporting a patient. Do not attempt locking mechanism troubleshooting while a patient is on the table.

steps.title: Visual Inspection of Locking Mechanism
steps.instructions: Examine the lock lever, pins, or mechanical parts for obstruction, debris, or visible damage. Clear any debris and ensure proper alignment. Expected outcome: unobstructed, smooth movement.

steps.title: Check Table Power
steps.instructions: Verify the table is powered on and battery or AC supply is functional. Locking actuators may require electrical power. Expected outcome: table powers normally.

steps.title: Inspect Control Panel / Handset Commands
steps.instructions: Attempt engaging the lock from the hand control or pendant. Observe indicator lights or audible clicks confirming activation. Expected outcome: lock engages or indicator responds.

steps.title: Confirm Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Ensure all safety interlocks are properly seated (e.g., brakes, caster locks, accessories). Many tables will not engage the lock if interlocks detect unsafe conditions. Expected outcome: lock engages when all interlocks satisfied.

steps.title: Test Actuator Function
steps.instructions: If safe, listen for actuator movement when lock command is given. No movement may indicate actuator or wiring issue. Expected outcome: audible actuator operation.

steps.title: Swap or Inspect Cables
steps.instructions: Check connections between control panel and lock actuator for loose or damaged cables. Swap with known-good cables if available. Expected outcome: lock functions normally with proper connection.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the table locking mechanism would not engage.
CCR.Cause: Safety interlocks were functional, actuator did not respond to lock command.
CCR.Resolution: Table removed from service and sent for repair; Out of Service label applied.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Mizuho OSI Trios Surgical Table System - Accessory Interface or Attachment Recognition Issues
description: Identifies causes of accessory or attachment recognition failure, including connection, interface, power, or software issues before assuming internal electronics failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not supporting a patient. No troubleshooting should occur while a patient is on the table.

steps.title: Inspect Physical Connections
steps.instructions: Check that all accessories and attachments are properly seated and locked in their ports. Loose or misaligned connectors often prevent recognition.

steps.title: Check Interface Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect cables connecting the table to accessory interfaces for damage, wear, or disconnection. Replace or reseat as needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Table
steps.instructions: Turn the table off, wait 30 seconds, and power it back on. Some recognition issues clear after a reset.

steps.title: Verify Accessory Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm that the attached accessory is compatible with the Trios system model. Non-compatible or defective attachments may not be recognized.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Look for blinking lights, LEDs, or display notifications on the table and accessory. They can indicate specific connection or interface faults.

steps.title: Swap Accessories or Ports
steps.instructions: If possible, try another known-working accessory in the same interface, or the same accessory in another compatible port, to isolate the issue.

CCR.Complaint: Table does not recognize attached accessory during procedure.
CCR.Cause: Accessory port or interface cable loose, or accessory incompatible.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated connections, verified compatible accessory; issue persisted, sent for service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights or error codes observed

title: Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table Surgical Table - Table Will Not Power On Or Battery Will Not Charge
description: Troubleshooting Hana table power failure, charging failure, weak battery operation, or loss of powered table movement.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the table is not supporting a patient before troubleshooting powered movement or charging problems.

If the table fails during a case, support the patient, stop table movement attempts, and follow OR escalation procedures.

Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before electrical or movement troubleshooting begins.

Why it matters: Surgical tables involve patient positioning, weight support, powered movement, and pinch-point hazards.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Condition
steps.instructions:
Confirm whether the issue is:

Table will not power on
Battery will not charge
Table powers on only when plugged in
Table powers on but dies quickly
Charger/power indicator does not illuminate
Hand control or pendant appears dead

Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or assemblies are suspected.

steps.title: Check That the Table Is Not in Active Use or Locked Out
steps.instructions:
Verify the table is parked safely, brakes are applied if applicable, and no clinical accessories or positioning components are interfering with access to power controls.

Expected outcome: Troubleshooting can be performed safely without unintended table movement.

steps.title: Check the AC Wall Outlet
steps.instructions:
Plug a known-good device or electrical safety tester into the same outlet used for charging the table.

Expected outcome: The outlet provides proper power.

If the outlet is dead, damaged, controlled by a wall switch, or connected to an inactive emergency power circuit, use a verified outlet and stop troubleshooting if charging resumes.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord
steps.instructions:
Check the table power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose strain relief, missing ground pin, or signs of heat damage.

Expected outcome: The cord is intact and safe to use.

If the cord is damaged, remove it from use and replace it with an approved cord before further troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Power Cord Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions:
Verify the power cord is firmly connected at the wall and at the table’s power inlet or charging connection.

Expected outcome: Charging or power indicators illuminate after a secure connection is made.

If the table begins charging after reseating the cord, document the loose connection and stop.

steps.title: Check for Charging Indicator Lights
steps.instructions:
With the table plugged into a verified outlet, observe any charge, power, battery, or status indicators.

Expected outcome: The table shows some indication that AC power is present or the battery is charging.

If there are no indicators, continue checking external power and connection points.

steps.title: Inspect the Table Power Inlet or Charging Port
steps.instructions:
Look for debris, bent contacts, loose connector fit, cracked housing, fluid contamination, or signs of overheating at the table’s power connection.

Expected outcome: The charging port is clean, dry, physically secure, and not heat-damaged.

If damage, fluid intrusion, or overheating is found, remove the table from service.

steps.title: Allow Time for a Severely Discharged Battery
steps.instructions:
If the table has been stored unplugged for an extended period, connect it to verified AC power and allow adequate charging time before retesting power-on.

Expected outcome: A deeply discharged battery may recover enough to power the control system after charging.

If the table still shows no charging activity or will not power on after a reasonable charge period, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the Main Power Switch or Enable Control
steps.instructions:
Verify the main power switch, system enable control, or battery power switch is in the correct operating position.

Expected outcome: The table powers on or displays normal standby/ready status.

If the table powers on after correcting the switch position, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Hand Control or Pendant Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the hand control is securely connected and that the connector is not loose, damaged, contaminated, or misaligned.

Expected outcome: The table recognizes the control device and responds normally.

Why it matters: A table may appear dead if the control interface is disconnected or not communicating.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Hand Control if Available
steps.instructions:
If the table has power indicators but no response from the controls, connect a known-good compatible hand control or pendant if available.

Expected outcome: The table responds normally with a known-good control.

If the table works with another control, tag the failed control for replacement and return the table only after full operational checks pass.

steps.title: Check for Emergency Stop or Lockout Condition
steps.instructions:
Verify any emergency stop, lockout, or safety disable function is not engaged.

Expected outcome: Once cleared, the table powers on and responds normally.

If the issue resolves, document the lockout condition and verify all table movements before returning to service.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid Intrusion or Cleaning Chemical Exposure
steps.instructions:
Look around the power inlet, control connectors, hand control, base, and battery/charging area for moisture, residue, or signs of liquid entry.

Expected outcome: No fluid contamination is present.

If fluid intrusion is suspected, remove the table from service. Do not continue powered testing.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Sounds or Smell
steps.instructions:
When connected to AC power, listen for clicking, buzzing, repeated relay cycling, or no activity at all. Check for unusual heat or electrical odor.

Expected outcome: No abnormal noise, heat, or smell is present.

If overheating, smoke, or electrical odor is detected, unplug the table immediately and remove it from service.

steps.title: Check Battery Behavior Under Load
steps.instructions:
If the table powers on but shuts down quickly when movement is attempted, observe whether the battery indicator drops rapidly or the table only works while plugged in.

Expected outcome: The battery supports normal table operation without immediate shutdown.

If the table only operates on AC power or the battery rapidly depletes, suspect battery failure or charging circuit failure after external causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Verify Charging After Power Cycle
steps.instructions:
Disconnect AC power, power the table off, wait briefly, reconnect AC power, and observe whether charging status changes.

Expected outcome: Charging status returns normally.

If the charging status remains absent or abnormal, continue to escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check if Power Is Restored
steps.instructions:
If the table powers on, test basic table functions without a patient and without load conditions that could create risk.

Confirm:

Power on/off operation
Battery indicator behavior
Charging indicator behavior
Hand control response
Table movement stops when controls are released
No abnormal noise, odor, heat, or hesitation

Expected outcome: The table powers on, charges normally, and responds safely.

If the table passes testing, return it to service according to facility policy.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported the Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table would not power on and the battery did not appear to charge when plugged in.
CCR.Cause: Verified outlet power was good, but the table showed no charging indication and would not power on after cord reseating and control connection checks.
CCR.Resolution: Removed table from service, labeled Out of Service, documented failed power/charging condition, and sent for bench evaluation of battery and charging system.

helpfulDetails: Whether table works on AC power only
Whether hand control was reseated or swapped
Any unusual sound, heat, or smell
Whether emergency stop or lockout was checked

title: Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table Surgical Table - Traction Spars Or Leg Supports Difficult To Adjust Or Lock
description: Troubleshooting traction spar, leg support, boot, or locking difficulty caused by positioning, debris, accessories, or mechanical resistance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot traction spars, leg supports, boots, or locking mechanisms while the patient is supported by the affected component. If the issue occurs during a case, notify clinical staff and follow facility procedure for safely supporting or transferring the patient before further troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is not placed at risk by an unstable traction or leg support component. Why it matters: Traction assemblies and leg supports directly affect limb positioning, patient stability, and surgical access.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff which component is difficult to adjust or lock: Traction spar; Traction boot; Leg support; Extension arm; Locking knob, clamp, lever, or release handle; Accessory rail or mounting point. Attempt the adjustment only when the table is unloaded and safe to manipulate. Expected outcome: The affected component and symptom are clearly identified. If the component adjusts and locks normally unloaded, stop and document that the issue could not be duplicated under safe test conditions.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Obstruction or Misalignment
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the traction spar, leg support, and mounting interface for drapes, straps, pads, cables, debris, or other accessories interfering with movement. Remove any obstruction and retry the adjustment. Expected outcome: The component moves freely and locks securely once interference is removed. If this resolves the issue, return the table to service after functional verification.

steps.title: Check Accessory Installation and Seating
steps.instructions: Verify that the traction spar, boot assembly, or leg support is fully seated in its mounting receiver or rail clamp. Remove and reinstall the accessory if safe to do so. Expected outcome: The accessory seats fully, aligns correctly, and locks without excessive force. Why it matters: Partially seated accessories may feel jammed, may not lock fully, or may bind during adjustment.

steps.title: Inspect Locking Knobs, Levers, and Release Mechanisms
steps.instructions: Check that the locking knob or lever is not already over-tightened, cross-loaded, or held under tension. Fully release the locking mechanism, reposition the component, then re-lock it using normal hand force only. Expected outcome: The mechanism releases, adjusts, and locks without forcing. If the lock holds correctly after proper release and reset, stop and document the correction.

steps.title: Check for Load or Position-Related Binding
steps.instructions: Confirm the traction spar or leg support is not being adjusted while under weight, side load, or traction force. Place the component in a neutral unloaded position and try the adjustment again. Expected outcome: The component moves more freely when unloaded and properly aligned. Why it matters: Traction assemblies can bind when adjusted under tension or when the limb support is not aligned with the mechanism.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid, Cleaning Residue, or Debris Buildup
steps.instructions: Look for dried prep solution, cleaner residue, surgical debris, tape adhesive, or corrosion around sliding surfaces, clamps, and locking points. Clean accessible external surfaces according to facility-approved cleaning practices. Expected outcome: External residue is removed and the component can be adjusted normally. Do not lubricate unless approved by the manufacturer or facility procedure. Unapproved lubricant can attract debris or affect locking reliability.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Matching Accessory
steps.instructions: If another compatible Hana traction spar, boot assembly, or leg support is available, compare fit and operation on the same table. Also test the suspect accessory on another compatible table only if safe and approved by local process. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the accessory or the table mounting location. Why it matters: This helps separate a table-side receiver problem from a damaged accessory.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Damage or Wear
steps.instructions: Check for bent spars, cracked handles, stripped knobs, loose hardware, worn clamps, damaged boot attachment points, or deformed mounting surfaces. Do not attempt to straighten, force, or modify the component. Expected outcome: No visible damage is present, or damaged parts are identified for repair. If damage is found, remove the affected component from service.

steps.title: Verify Locking Security After Adjustment
steps.instructions: Once adjusted, apply a controlled, reasonable manual check to confirm the traction spar or leg support remains secure. Do not use excessive force or simulate patient loading beyond normal safe handling. Expected outcome: The component remains locked and does not slip, rotate, or loosen. If the component slips or will not hold position, remove it from service.

steps.title: Check Table Configuration and Compatible Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm the accessory belongs with the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table and is not from another table system or incompatible setup. Verify staff are not mixing boots, spars, clamps, adapters, or support components from different systems. Expected outcome: The table and accessory combination is correct. If an incompatible accessory is identified, remove it and provide the correct compatible component.

steps.title: Perform Final Operational Check
steps.instructions: With the table unloaded, verify the affected traction or leg support assembly can: Install correctly; Adjust through its intended range; Lock securely; Release without sticking; Be removed without excessive force. Expected outcome: The component operates smoothly and locks reliably. If the final check passes, return the table or accessory to service and document findings.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported that the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table left traction spar was difficult to adjust and would not consistently lock during setup.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found dried residue and minor debris around the traction spar locking interface, preventing smooth adjustment.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned accessible locking surfaces, reinstalled the traction spar, verified full adjustment range and secure locking, and returned the table to service.

helpfulDetails: Which traction spar, boot, or leg support was affected
Whether the issue occurred loaded or unloaded
Whether the component was difficult to move, difficult to release, or would not lock
Any visible damage, bent parts, cracked handles, or worn knobs
Whether residue, debris, or obstruction was found
Whether the accessory was removed and reinstalled
Whether a known-good accessory was tested
Whether the issue followed the accessory or stayed with the table
Whether the device was returned to service or sent for repair
Alarm behavior, if any alarm or staff warning was associated with the issue
Power behavior, if table power or powered movement affected the setup
Environmental factors such as cleaning residue, drapes, straps, pads, cables, or debris
Indicator lights, if any related table or control indicators were present

title: Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table Surgical Table - Table Movement Failure From Pendant, Control Box, Or Actuator Fault
description: Troubleshooting Hana table movement failure caused by pendant, control box, power, connection, lockout, or actuator-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table is in use with a patient, do not continue troubleshooting unless the patient is fully supported and clinical leadership confirms it is safe. If movement is needed during a procedure and the table does not respond, notify the OR team immediately and follow facility procedure for patient support, transfer, or alternate positioning. Expected outcome: Patient positioning remains stable and safe before any troubleshooting continues. Why it matters: Surgical table movement failure can create immediate patient safety risks, especially during orthopedic positioning.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Movement Failure
steps.instructions: Identify exactly what is not working: No movement from the pendant; One specific movement does not work; Multiple movements fail; Table responds from one control source but not another; Movement starts then stops; Actuator makes noise but does not move; No sound or response at all. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to a specific control path, movement function, or general power/control problem. If the issue is confirmed and repeatable, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect around the table base, leg spars, traction components, accessory mounts, and patient support surfaces for anything blocking motion. Remove any item that could mechanically restrict movement, including cords, OR drapes, accessory clamps, floor objects, or improperly positioned attachments. Expected outcome: Table movement resumes if an external obstruction was preventing travel. If this resolves the issue, stop and document the obstruction found.

steps.title: Verify Table Power Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is connected to appropriate facility power if required for the movement function being tested. Check that the power cord is fully seated at the wall outlet and table power inlet, if applicable. Verify that power indicators, control box lights, or display indicators are present. Expected outcome: The table has confirmed power available for powered movement. If power is restored and movement returns, stop and document the power issue.

steps.title: Test the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug a known-good electrical safety analyzer, outlet tester, or approved test device into the same outlet. Confirm the outlet is energized and not controlled by a wall switch, emergency power transfer issue, tripped breaker, or failed receptacle. Expected outcome: The outlet provides stable power. If the outlet fails testing, remove the table from use until power is corrected or move the table to a verified outlet.

steps.title: Check Battery or Charge Status, If Applicable
steps.instructions: If the table uses battery-supported movement, check battery charge indicators or low-power messages. Connect the table to AC power and allow charging per facility practice before retesting movement. Expected outcome: Low battery or depleted charge is ruled out as the cause. If movement returns after charging, document low battery or insufficient charge as the cause.

steps.title: Inspect the Pendant for Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the pendant for: Cracked housing; Stuck buttons; Fluid contamination; Missing button covers; Damaged cable strain relief; Bent or loose connector pins; Intermittent response when the cable is gently repositioned. Do not use excessive force on the connector or cable. Expected outcome: The pendant is physically intact and not obviously damaged. If the pendant is damaged, remove the table from service or replace the pendant if your department stocks approved replacement parts.

steps.title: Reseat the Pendant Connection
steps.instructions: Power the table down if safe and appropriate. Disconnect and reconnect the pendant cable at the control box or table connection point. Make sure the connector is fully seated and properly aligned. Retest the affected movement. Expected outcome: Movement resumes if the issue was caused by a loose or poorly seated pendant connection. If this resolves the issue, stop and document the loose pendant connection.

steps.title: Check for Pendant Button Lockout or Control Mode Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm that no control lockout, emergency stop, pendant disable setting, or alternate control mode is active. Look for indicator lights, disabled movement functions, or button combinations that may prevent pendant control. Expected outcome: The pendant is enabled and allowed to command table movement. If control lockout was active and clearing it restores movement, stop and document the setting found.

steps.title: Test Alternate Controls, If Available
steps.instructions: If the Hana table has another approved control point, such as a control box interface or secondary control, test the same movement from that control. Compare results: Works from alternate control but not pendant: suspect pendant, pendant cable, or pendant connection. Fails from all controls: suspect power, control box, actuator, limit switch, or internal control issue. Only one movement fails: suspect function-specific actuator, cable, limit, or mechanical issue. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to either the pendant/control input path or the movement hardware path.

steps.title: Inspect the Control Box Area
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the control box and external cable connections for: Loose connectors; Damaged cable jackets; Pulled strain reliefs; Fluid intrusion; Cracked housing; Burnt smell; Unusual heat; Missing or abnormal indicator lights. Do not open the control box or perform board-level troubleshooting. Expected outcome: External control box condition is verified. If there is heat, odor, fluid intrusion, or visible damage, remove the table from service immediately.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop or Safety Interlock Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that any emergency stop, brake, lock, or safety interlock is not engaged. Verify the table is not in a condition that intentionally prevents movement, such as an unsafe configuration, accessory conflict, or end-of-travel limit. Expected outcome: Movement is not being blocked by a safety condition. If clearing the safety condition restores movement, stop and document the interlock or configuration issue.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Response
steps.instructions: While commanding the failed movement, listen for: No sound at all; Relay click but no movement; Motor/actuator noise without motion; Grinding, binding, or clicking; Movement that starts then stops. Expected outcome: The table response helps separate a control-command issue from a mechanical or actuator issue. Why it matters: A silent actuator may indicate no command or no power to that function. A noisy actuator without movement may indicate binding, actuator failure, or mechanical load issue.

steps.title: Check for Binding or Overload
steps.instructions: Remove nonessential accessories if safe and clinically appropriate. Confirm the table is not overloaded, unevenly loaded, or restricted by traction accessories, leg spars, boots, transfer boards, or floor contact. Retest movement with no patient present and only approved minimum accessories installed. Expected outcome: Movement resumes if the table was restricted by load, accessory position, or binding. If the problem only occurs under load, remove the table from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Test Other Movement Functions
steps.instructions: Test other powered functions one at a time. Determine whether the failure affects: One actuator only; One direction only; All powered functions; Pendant control only; Control box output generally. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is clearly identified for repair escalation. If only one function fails and other movements work normally, suspect that movement’s actuator path, limit switch, wiring, or mechanical linkage.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Table
steps.instructions: If safe and no patient is on the table, power the table off, disconnect from AC power if applicable, wait briefly, reconnect power, and restart. Retest the failed movement. Expected outcome: Temporary control logic lockup is cleared. If movement returns after power cycling, perform additional functional checks before returning the table to service. Document the reset behavior clearly.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Safety Check
steps.instructions: If the table movement returns, verify all required table movements operate smoothly and stop correctly. Confirm the pendant responds consistently, the control box does not show abnormal indicators, and no movement is intermittent. Expected outcome: The table operates normally and safely before return to service. If any movement remains intermittent or unreliable, do not return the table to service.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported the Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table would not move when commands were entered from the pendant.
CCR.Cause: Pendant connection was found partially seated at the control box, preventing reliable movement commands.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated pendant connector, verified pendant response, tested table movement functions, and returned the table to service after successful operational checks.

helpfulDetails: Whether the table was occupied or unloaded during troubleshooting
Battery or charge status, if applicable
Any binding, obstruction, or accessory interference found
Any unusual heat, smell, fluid intrusion, or visible damage

title: Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table Surgical Table - Accessory Rail, Boot, or Traction Component Loose / Damaged
description: Troubleshooting loose, damaged, or insecure Hana table rails, boots, traction boots, traction spars, clamps, or related orthopedic accessories.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the table while it is supporting an active patient unless the situation is urgent and clinically controlled. If looseness is noticed during setup or a procedure, notify clinical staff immediately and support the patient or affected limb according to facility protocol. Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before any table or traction accessory is adjusted. Why it matters: Loose traction components or boots can affect limb positioning, traction accuracy, and patient safety.

steps.title: Remove the Table or Accessory From Use if Safety Is Questionable
steps.instructions: If the accessory rail, boot, traction assembly, perineal post, clamp, or spar appears unstable, remove the table or affected accessory from clinical use. Label the table or accessory Out of Service if it cannot be safely secured. Expected outcome: Staff do not continue using a table setup with questionable mechanical stability.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Component
steps.instructions: Identify exactly what was reported loose or damaged: Accessory rail; Boot; Traction boot; Boot clamp; Traction spar; Traction carriage; Perineal post or post adapter; Rail-mounted accessory; Locking knob, lever, latch, or clamp. Expected outcome: The problem area is clearly identified before adjustments are attempted. Why it matters: Hana table positioning issues can involve the table frame, rail interface, traction hardware, or removable accessory components.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the reported component for cracks, bent hardware, stripped threads, missing pads, loose fasteners, broken handles, damaged knobs, worn locking teeth, or deformed mounting surfaces. Expected outcome: No obvious damage is present, or damaged parts are identified for removal from service. If cracks, bending, broken welds, missing hardware, or stripped locking parts are found, stop troubleshooting and escalate for repair or replacement.

steps.title: Check That the Correct Accessory Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm that the boot, clamp, traction component, or rail accessory is correct for the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table and is not from another table system. Expected outcome: The accessory is compatible with the table and intended mounting location. Why it matters: Similar-looking orthopedic table accessories may not lock safely if used on the wrong table or rail interface.

steps.title: Verify Proper Seating of the Accessory
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the loose accessory using normal external setup methods. Confirm that the accessory is fully seated, flush, and aligned before tightening any locking knob or clamp. Expected outcome: The accessory seats fully and locks securely without excessive force. If reseating corrects the looseness, document the setup issue and return the device or accessory to service only after confirming stability.

steps.title: Check Locking Knobs, Levers, and Clamps
steps.instructions: Inspect all user-accessible locking points for proper engagement. Tighten only as intended by normal operation. Do not force damaged, slipping, or binding hardware. Expected outcome: Locking controls engage smoothly and hold the accessory firmly. If a knob spins freely, bottoms out without clamping, will not tighten, or slips under hand pressure, remove the accessory from service.

steps.title: Check for Missing or Worn Interface Hardware
steps.instructions: Look for missing bushings, pads, washers, pins, retaining clips, clamp inserts, or protective surfaces at the rail or traction interface. Expected outcome: All visible interface parts are present and properly positioned. Why it matters: Missing small parts can prevent the accessory from clamping securely even when the main locking knob feels tight.

steps.title: Inspect the Accessory Rail Area
steps.instructions: Check the rail for looseness, deformation, missing screws, visible gaps, damaged rail edges, or signs of impact. Gently verify whether the rail itself moves relative to the table frame. Expected outcome: The rail is secure and shows no visible damage. If the rail is loose, bent, cracked, or separated from the table structure, remove the table from service and escalate.

steps.title: Check the Traction Assembly for Play or Slippage
steps.instructions: For traction-related complaints, check the traction spar, boot connection, traction carriage, and locking mechanism for excessive movement. Apply only light manual force during inspection. Do not simulate clinical traction loads beyond safe handling. Expected outcome: The traction assembly remains secure and does not slip, rotate unexpectedly, or wobble at the locking point. If movement persists after proper setup, remove the accessory from service.

steps.title: Inspect the Boot and Patient Contact Areas
steps.instructions: Check the boot shell, padding, straps, buckles, attachment hardware, and connection point to the traction assembly. Expected outcome: The boot is intact, clean, properly padded, and securely attaches to the traction system. If the boot is cracked, has damaged straps, missing padding, or unreliable retention, remove it from use.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Accessory if Available
steps.instructions: Install a known-good boot, clamp, or traction component on the same table location. If the known-good accessory locks securely, the original accessory is likely defective. If multiple accessories are loose at the same table location, the issue may involve the table rail or mounting interface. Expected outcome: The fault is isolated to either the accessory or the table.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Debris at the Mounting Interface
steps.instructions: Inspect the rail, clamp, and traction connection for fluid residue, surgical debris, tape buildup, cleaning residue, or foreign material. Clean only according to approved facility and manufacturer-compatible cleaning practices. Expected outcome: The mounting surfaces are clean and allow full engagement. If cleaning and reseating restores secure locking, document the finding and verify safe operation before return to service.

steps.title: Perform a Final Stability Check
steps.instructions: After any reseating or accessory swap, verify the component remains secure through normal range of setup movement. Confirm there is no unexpected wobble, sliding, rotation, or failure of the locking control. Expected outcome: The table accessory is secure and safe for clinical setup. If the issue is resolved, document the corrective action and return the table or accessory to service.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table traction boot was loose during orthopedic case setup.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the boot clamp would not hold securely after proper reseating, and a known-good boot locked correctly on the same traction spar.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the damaged traction boot from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented failed clamp retention, and sent it for repair or replacement evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Table removed from service or accessory only removed from service
Rail, boot, clamp, spar, or traction carriage location
Whether the accessory was fully seated
Visible cracks, bending, stripped threads, or missing hardware
Whether a known-good accessory was tested
Whether the rail itself was loose or damaged
Cleaning residue, debris, or contamination found

title: Mizuho OSI Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table Surgical Table - Hydraulic, Lift, or Trendelenburg Movement Weak, Uneven, or Noisy
description: Troubleshooting weak, uneven, noisy, or unreliable table lift, hydraulic, or Trendelenburg movement before escalating for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot table movement with a patient on the table unless the issue is minor and movement is not required for immediate safety. If movement is weak, uneven, jerky, noisy, or unpredictable during a case, notify clinical staff and support the patient according to OR protocol. Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before any troubleshooting begins. If the table cannot move safely or predictably, remove it from service after the procedure or replace it before use.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Movement Issue
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff which movement is affected: Table lift up or down; Trendelenburg; Reverse Trendelenburg; Lateral tilt, if applicable; Intermittent movement; Noise during movement; Uneven or drifting movement. Operate the table only when safe and unloaded, if possible. Expected outcome: The exact symptom is confirmed instead of treating the issue as a general table failure.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction Around the Table
steps.instructions: Inspect around the base, column, floor locks, traction attachments, leg spars, accessories, and nearby equipment. Look for cables, foot pedals, arm boards, traction components, drapes, equipment carts, or floor debris interfering with motion. Expected outcome: The table moves normally after the obstruction is removed. If this resolves the issue, return the table to service after confirming full movement range.

steps.title: Verify the Table Is on a Suitable Surface
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is positioned on a stable, level floor and is not partially sitting on a floor seam, cable cover, mat, threshold, or debris. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth and balanced when the base is properly supported. Why it matters: Uneven floor support can make movement appear weak, noisy, or uneven even when the hydraulic system is functioning.

steps.title: Check Floor Lock or Brake Engagement
steps.instructions: Confirm the table base is properly locked according to normal clinical use expectations. A table that is not fully stabilized may shift, bind, or make unusual noise during lift or Trendelenburg movement. Expected outcome: Movement improves once the table is fully stabilized. If floor locks do not engage properly, remove the table from service.

steps.title: Verify Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the table has adequate power or battery charge, depending on the configuration in use. Check for low battery indicators, charger connection issues, missing power, damaged power cord, or a table that has been stored unplugged. Expected outcome: Movement returns to normal after charging or restoring proper power. Why it matters: Weak or slow movement can occur when available power is insufficient for motorized or hydraulic operation.

steps.title: Inspect the Hand Control and Pendant Cable
steps.instructions: Check the hand control for damaged buttons, cracked housing, stuck controls, liquid contamination, or a loose cable connection. Operate the affected movement from the normal control device and compare with any alternate approved controls if available. Expected outcome: The movement works normally with a known-good control or after reseating the connection. If the table behaves differently with another control, the control device or connection should be evaluated.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Load-Related Binding
steps.instructions: Inspect the Hana table attachments, traction boots, spars, perineal post area, leg supports, extension components, and orthopedic accessories. Confirm accessories are installed correctly and not creating mechanical interference during table articulation. Expected outcome: Movement becomes smooth after correcting accessory position or removing the interfering accessory. Why it matters: Orthopedic table attachments can create binding that may be mistaken for hydraulic weakness.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Hydraulic or Mechanical Noise
steps.instructions: Operate the affected function briefly while unloaded and safe. Listen for: Grinding; Squealing; Pump strain; Clicking; Chatter; Air-like bubbling noise; Sudden thumps or drops. Expected outcome: Normal table movement should be controlled and consistent without excessive noise. If abnormal noise is present, stop testing and remove the table from service.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid Leakage
steps.instructions: Check under and around the table base, column, hydraulic lift area, actuator areas, and nearby floor. Look for oil, dampness, stains, residue, or fluid trails. Expected outcome: No leakage is found. If hydraulic fluid leakage is present or suspected, remove the table from service immediately and label it Out of Service.

steps.title: Check for Drift or Failure to Hold Position
steps.instructions: Raise the table or place it in the reported position only if safe and unloaded. Observe whether it holds position or slowly lowers, tilts, or drifts. Expected outcome: The table maintains position without drifting. If the table drifts, sinks, or cannot hold Trendelenburg position, remove it from service. Why it matters: Failure to hold position is a patient safety concern and may indicate hydraulic or mechanical failure.

steps.title: Check Movement Through a Limited Safe Range
steps.instructions: Operate the affected function through a short range first, then a fuller range only if the table moves smoothly. Do not force movement if the table hesitates, binds, stalls, or makes worsening noise. Expected outcome: Movement is smooth, even, and repeatable. If movement is weak, uneven, jerky, noisy, or inconsistent after external checks, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Perform a Final Operational Check
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, verify the table can perform the affected movement repeatedly without unusual sound, drift, delay, or uneven motion. Confirm the table is clean, dry, stable, and ready for clinical use. Expected outcome: The table operates normally and can be returned to service. If the issue returns during testing, remove the table from service.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported the Hana Orthopedic Surgery Table had weak and noisy Trendelenburg movement during setup.
CCR.Cause: Found table movement remained uneven after checking power, hand control connection, floor lock engagement, accessories, and surrounding obstructions.
CCR.Resolution: Removed table from service, labeled Out of Service, documented suspected internal hydraulic or lift system fault, and escalated for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Movement affected: lift, Trendelenburg, reverse Trendelenburg, or tilt
Whether issue occurred loaded or unloaded
Noise type and when it occurred
Whether the table drifted or failed to hold position
